items.title: BRCA1/2 Mutations and Cancer Risk Management
items.content: Women with mutations in BRCA1 or BRCA2 have a significantly increased risk (five- to twentyfold) of developing breast and ovarian cancer. Genetic counseling is recommended, along with specific screening and prevention strategies such as prophylactic oophorectomy and breast MRI for those at high risk.
keyPoints: BRCA1/2 mutations increase breast and ovarian cancer risk five- to twentyfold.
Genetic counseling is advised for individuals with these mutations.
Prophylactic interventions include oophorectomy and breast MRI, which are not recommended for average-risk women due to potential harm.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.title: Polygenic Risk Scores in Predictive Medicine
items.content: Polygenic risk scores, which combine information from variants across hundreds of genes, are being evaluated for identifying individuals at high risk of coronary heart disease and other conditions. The use of whole exome/whole genome sequencing is expected to enhance the application of these tests in clinical practice.
keyPoints: Polygenic risk scores assess risk based on multiple gene variants.
These scores are under evaluation for predicting coronary heart disease risk.
Whole exome/whole genome sequencing may accelerate the use of polygenic risk scores in preventive care.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.title: Use of Imaging Data in Risk-Stratified Prevention
items.content: Imaging data, such as coronary CT scans, are increasingly used to inform decisions about preventive interventions like statin therapy, especially when other risk assessments provide conflicting information.
keyPoints: Coronary CT scans help guide decisions on starting statin therapy in preventive cardiology.
Imaging data can predict both benefits and potential harms of screening or prevention.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.title: Why Effective Treatment is Crucial for Screening Success
items.content: Screening programs are most effective when there is an available and effective treatment for the early stages of a disease. This principle ensures that detecting a condition leads to better health outcomes.
keyPoints: Effective screening requires availability of treatment for detected conditions.
Challenges exist in diseases like Alzheimer's where no curative treatments are available, limiting screening effectiveness.
Screening strategies may differ based on the presence and efficacy of early-stage treatments.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.title: How Latent Disease Phases Enhance Screening Efficacy
items.content: Screening is most beneficial when the disease has a latent or preclinical phase during which early intervention can prevent progression to more severe stages.
keyPoints: A long latent phase allows for interventions that increase cure chances, as seen in many cancers like colon cancer with polypectomy.
Early detection of conditions such as hypertension and hyperlipidemia enables preventive measures reducing cardiovascular risks.
Screening effectiveness is reduced when the preclinical phase is short or diseases are detected at an incurable stage.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.title: Balancing Cost and Benefit in Screening Programs
items.content: The economic costs of screening must be justified by the health benefits, considering both direct medical expenditures and broader societal impacts.
keyPoints: Screening should balance cost with overall medical expenditure and potential health improvements.
Low-dose chest CT scanning reduces lung cancer mortality by 20% in high-risk individuals (30-pack-year smoking history).
Economic considerations are crucial, especially when resources are limited or alternative strategies may offer better outcomes.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.title: Understanding Absolute and Relative Impact in Screening
items.content: When evaluating the effectiveness of screening programs, it is crucial to consider both absolute and relative impacts on disease incidence or mortality. This helps in comparing the benefits across different preventive services.
keyPoints: Absolute difference allows comparison of benefit size; e.g., ~1.2 fewer deaths per 1000 from breast cancer with mammography over 12 years.
Colon cancer screening with annual FOBT saves ~3 lives per 1000 over 13 years, and 20–24 lives per 1000 over 25 years.
Relative impact of FOBT is a 30% reduction in colon cancer death; mammography shows a 14–32% reduction in breast cancer death.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.title: Calculating the Number Needed to Screen (NNS)
items.content: The number needed to screen (NNS) is a metric used to determine how many individuals need to be screened or receive an intervention to prevent one death, providing insight into the efficiency of screening programs.
keyPoints: Inverse of absolute mortality difference gives NNS; e.g., 731 women aged 65–69 need DEXA screening to prevent one hip fracture.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.title: Evaluating Increase in Life Expectancy from Screening
items.content: Screening and preventive interventions aim to increase average life expectancy for a population, though the benefit is not uniform across individuals. A small subset of patients may experience significant gains.
keyPoints: Average life expectancy gain applies to populations, not individuals.
Pap smears add up to 25 years for the 2% who would develop cervical cancer.
A reasonable goal for population-based strategies is a 1-month increase in life expectancy.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.subtopic: ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION
items.title: Understanding False Positives and Overdiagnosis in Screening Tests
items.content: Screening tests can lead to false-positive results, which may cause unnecessary anxiety or interventions. False positives vary by test type: for mammography and chest CT, they involve identifying non-malignant abnormalities; for Pap smears, they detect potentially premalignant states that rarely progress to cancer. Overdiagnosis is a significant concern, as it involves detecting diseases unlikely to present clinically during the patient's lifetime.
keyPoints: False-positive results are common in screening tests and vary by test type.
Overdiagnosis may occur with 15–40% of breast cancers identified by mammography and 15–37% of prostate cancers identified by PSA testing.
Long-term follow-up is required to accurately assess overdiagnosis.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION
items.title: Evaluating the Risks and Benefits of Radiation Exposure in Screening Tests
items.content: Screening tests involving radiation, such as mammography and chest CT, contribute to cumulative radiation exposure. While individual test radiation doses are small, repeated exposures from multiple sources raise concerns about long-term impacts.
keyPoints: Radiation exposure is a concern with screening tests like mammography and chest CT.
Individual radiation doses from these tests are very small.
The cumulative impact of repeated radiation exposure is still being determined.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION

items.title: Cost-Effectiveness in Screening Strategies
items.content: When evaluating population-based screening strategies, it is crucial to assess cost-effectiveness by calculating the cost per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY). This involves considering not only the direct costs of interventions but also indirect costs such as time away from work and adverse events. A strategy costing $50,000–100,000 per QALY is deemed 'cost-effective'.
keyPoints: Cost-effectiveness assessed by cost per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY).
Includes direct intervention costs and indirect costs like time away from work.
$50,000–100,000 per QALY considered 'cost-effective'.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · WEIGHING THE BENEFITS AND HARMS

items.title: Role of USPSTF in Screening Recommendations
items.content: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) provides evidence-based recommendations for screening and preventive strategies by evaluating the benefit-to-harm ratio. While there is consensus on some screenings like hyperlipidemia and colorectal cancer, agreement varies for others such as breast cancer in women in their forties and prostate cancer. Recommendations are updated periodically, reflecting available data at the time.
keyPoints: USPSTF provides evidence-based screening recommendations.
Consensus on screenings like hyperlipidemia and colorectal cancer.
Varying agreement for breast cancer (women in their forties) and prostate cancer.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · WEIGHING THE BENEFITS AND HARMS

items.title: Shared Decision-Making in Preventive Services
items.content: In situations where the benefit-to-harm ratio of preventive services is uncertain, shared decision-making becomes crucial. This approach involves individualized discussions to align screening decisions with personal values and risk tolerance. Examples include prostate cancer screening and choosing colon cancer screening techniques like FOBT, flexible sigmoidoscopy, or colonoscopy, each with varying benefits, costs, and risks.
keyPoints: Shared decision-making important when benefit-to-harm ratio is uncertain.
Examples: Prostate cancer screening and choice of colon cancer screening technique.
FOBT reduces deaths by 15–30%, flexible sigmoidoscopy by ~40–60%, colonoscopy by ~50–70% under optimal conditions.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · WEIGHING THE BENEFITS AND HARMS

items.subtopic: COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING
items.title: The Critical Role of Counseling in Preventive Health Care
items.content: Counseling by health care providers plays a significant role in effecting health behavior changes, particularly in areas such as tobacco cessation, alcohol use management, diet, and exercise. These factors are major contributors to preventable deaths in developed countries.
keyPoints: Tobacco and alcohol use, diet, and exercise influence the majority of preventable deaths.
Counseling by health care providers is supported by evidence for effecting behavior change.
Routine counseling includes physical activity and injury prevention (e.g., seat belts, helmets).
Preventing and treating excess weight is a top priority due to obesity complications.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING

items.subtopic: COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING
items.title: Optimizing Disease Prevention and Screening in Practice
items.content: Implementing disease prevention and screening strategies can be enhanced through the use of electronic health records, patient access to medical records, standing orders for immunizations, and age-specific tools developed by the USPSTF. Integrating preventive measures during visits for chronic illness management is a practical approach.
keyPoints: Electronic health records with reminder systems help track and meet guidelines.
Patient access to medical records can enhance adherence to screening.
Standing orders are effective for immunizations.
Age-specific USPSTF tools assist in implementing screenings.
Preventive measures can be incorporated during chronic illness management visits.
Screening benefits typically accrue over 5–10 years, with limited data supporting continued screening past age 75.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING

items.question: Which type of genetic mutation is associated with a markedly increased risk of breast and ovarian cancer?
options: Mutations in BRCA1 or BRCA2 genes
Variants in low-penetrance genes
Polygenic risk scores
Proteomics data
items.explanation: Mutations in either BRCA1 or BRCA2 significantly increase the risk of breast and ovarian cancer.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.question: What is a primary goal of health care related to disease prevention?
options: To treat diseases after symptoms appear
To prevent or detect diseases early enough for effective intervention
To focus on symptomatic individuals only
To provide genetic counseling universally
items.explanation: The primary goal is to prevent disease or detect it early enough that intervention will be more effective.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.question: Which preventive intervention might be recommended for women with BRCA mutations?
options: Routine mammography only
Prophylactic oophorectomy or breast MRI
No specific interventions beyond standard care
Regular blood pressure monitoring
items.explanation: Women with BRCA mutations may be recommended prophylactic oophorectomy and breast MRI.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.question: What is a key consideration when recommending screening tests or preventive interventions?
options: They should be administered reactively in response to symptoms
Evidence must show they are both practical and effective
They can be recommended without evidence of effectiveness
Only high-risk individuals should receive them
items.explanation: Recommendations require a high bar of evidence that testing and intervention are both practical and effective.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a basic principle of screening according to the World Health Organization?
options: The condition should be an important health problem.
There should be a latent stage of the disease.
Screening should always lead to improved outcomes for all diseases.
The cost of finding a case should be balanced in relation to overall medical expenditure.
items.explanation: The statement that screening should always lead to improved outcomes is not listed as a basic principle; effectiveness depends on various factors including the disease's natural history and available treatments.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.question: What is one reason why screening for lung cancer has historically been challenging?
options: Most tumors are curable by the time they can be detected on a chest x-ray.
There is no latent stage in lung cancer.
Most tumors are not curable by the time they can be detected on a chest x-ray.
Screening tests for lung cancer are highly invasive.
items.explanation: Historically, most lung tumors were not curable by the time detection occurred via chest x-ray, making early screening less effective.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.question: Which condition is an example where a 'see and treat' approach has been used in some countries?
options: Hypertension
Cervical cancer
Alzheimer's disease
Prostate cancer
items.explanation: 'See and treat' approaches are currently used for cervical cancer screening in some countries, addressing the challenge of diagnosis and treatment facilities.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.question: Why might early detection of prostate cancer not lead to a difference in mortality rate?
options: Prostate cancer is always aggressive.
Early detection invariably leads to over-treatment.
The disease is often indolent and competing morbidities may cause mortality.
There are no effective treatments for early-stage prostate cancer.
items.explanation: Prostate cancer is often indolent, meaning it progresses slowly, and other conditions like coronary artery disease might be the actual cause of death.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.question: Which study design is considered the gold standard for demonstrating the efficacy of screening tests and preventive interventions?
options: Observational studies
Randomized controlled trials (RCTs)
Ecologic data
Case-control designs
items.explanation: Randomized controlled trials with mortality outcomes are considered the gold standard.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.question: What is lead time bias in the context of screening tests?
options: Screening identifies rapidly progressive disease more frequently.
Screening creates a perception of longer survival by diagnosing earlier.
Screening reduces mortality but not incidence.
Screening increases disease-free survival without affecting mortality.
items.explanation: Lead time bias occurs because screening identifies a case before it would have presented clinically, thereby creating the perception that a patient lived longer after diagnosis simply by moving the date of diagnosis earlier rather than the date of death later.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.question: Which endpoint is critical to assess when evaluating the effectiveness of screening?
options: Length of disease survival
Disease incidence or mortality
Number of subjects screened
Increase in average life expectancy
items.explanation: It is critical that disease incidence or mortality is the primary endpoint to avoid biases like lead time and length time bias.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.question: What does a relative impact of FOBT (30% reduction in colon cancer death) emphasize when compared with mammography?
options: The absolute number of lives saved is more important.
Both relative and absolute comparisons are important.
Mammography has a greater impact on mortality reduction.
FOBT is less effective than mammography.
items.explanation: Despite the higher absolute benefit of colon cancer screening, both relative and absolute impacts should be considered for comprehensive evaluation.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.subtopic: ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION
items.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a potential harm associated with screening and prevention?
options: Side effects from preventive medications
Increased risk of developing new diseases
False-positive screening tests
Overdiagnosis of disease
items.explanation: The passage does not list an increased risk of developing new diseases as a harm associated with screening and prevention.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION
items.question: What is the primary concern regarding side effects from currently recommended vaccinations?
options: They are primarily limited to discomfort and minor immune reactions.
They include severe allergic reactions in most cases.
They lead to long-term adverse consequences.
They are associated with a high incidence of serious adverse outcomes.
items.explanation: The passage states that side effects from currently recommended vaccinations are primarily limited to discomfort and minor immune reactions.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION

items.subtopic: ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION
items.question: What is the estimated range of breast cancers identified by mammography screening that may never have presented clinically?
items.explanation: The passage estimates that as much as 15–40% of breast cancers identified by mammography screening may never have presented clinically.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION

items.question: Which factor is NOT typically considered when assessing the cost-effectiveness of a screening strategy?
options: Cost per year of life saved
Quality-adjusted life-year (QALY)
Time away from work due to intervention
Number of patients screened
items.explanation: The passage discusses cost-effectiveness in terms of cost per QALY and other factors, but not the number of patients screened.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · WEIGHING THE BENEFITS AND HARMS

items.question: What is considered 'cost-effective' for strategies according to typical assessments?
options: $10,000–25,000 per quality-adjusted year of life saved
$50,000–100,000 per quality-adjusted year of life saved
$200,000–300,000 per quality-adjusted year of life saved
$500,000–1,000,000 per quality-adjusted year of life saved
items.explanation: Strategies costing $50,000–100,000 per QALY are typically considered cost-effective.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · WEIGHING THE BENEFITS AND HARMS

items.question: Which organization provides evidence-based recommendations for screening and preventive strategies?
options: World Health Organization (WHO)
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF)
American Medical Association (AMA)
items.explanation: The USPSTF is mentioned as providing evidence-based recommendations.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · WEIGHING THE BENEFITS AND HARMS

items.question: When should screening for colon cancer be initiated in individuals with a significant family history of the disease?
options: At age 50, regardless of family history
10 years before the youngest family member was diagnosed
5 years after the youngest family member was diagnosed
Only if symptoms are present
items.explanation: Screening should start 10 years before the age at which the youngest family member was diagnosed.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · WEIGHING THE BENEFITS AND HARMS

items.subtopic: COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING
items.question: Which preventive health care measure is considered the single greatest according to the passage?
options: Helping patients quit smoking
Encouraging weight loss
Promoting exercise
Reducing alcohol consumption
items.explanation: The passage states that helping patients quit smoking is perhaps the single greatest preventive health care measure.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING

items.subtopic: COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING
items.question: What role do educational campaigns, public policy changes, and community-based interventions play in preventive strategies?
options: They are the primary methods for disease prevention.
They have no significant impact on health behavior change.
They are important parts of a strategy to address preventable factors.
They replace the need for individual counseling by healthcare providers.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that these interventions are important parts of a strategy for addressing preventive factors in some settings.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING

items.subtopic: COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING
items.question: What is the role of electronic health records in implementing disease prevention and screening strategies?
options: They are used to store patient medical histories.
They provide reminder systems to help physicians track and meet guidelines.
They replace the need for nurse involvement in immunizations.
They eliminate the necessity for age-specific recommendations.
items.explanation: The passage states that an appropriately configured electronic health record can provide reminder systems that make it easier for physicians to track and meet guidelines.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING

items.statement: Proteomics data have been successfully developed into reliable, predictive measures for disease risk.
items.explanation: Developing reliable, predictive measures using proteomics has proven challenging.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.statement: Coronary CT scans are used to inform decisions about starting statin therapy in preventive cardiology.
items.explanation: Coronary CT scans help decide on beginning statin therapy when other risk assessments are uncertain.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.statement: Screening is most effective when applied to relatively common disorders that carry a large disease burden.
items.explanation: Screening targets conditions with significant health impact and prevalence, such as heart diseases and cancers.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.explanation: Currently, there are no curative treatments for Alzheimer's disease, even with early detection.

items.statement: Lead time bias can create the appearance of improved disease survival without an actual effect.
items.explanation: Lead time bias occurs when screening identifies a case earlier, creating the perception of longer survival.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.statement: Disease-free survival is not considered as an alternative endpoint in assessing screening benefits.
items.explanation: Disease-free survival is indeed considered, especially with newer therapies controlling metastatic cancers.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · METHODS OF MEASURING HEALTH BENEFITS

items.subtopic: ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION
items.statement: Screening tests involving radiation always lead to significant long-term adverse consequences.
items.explanation: The passage notes that the absolute amount of radiation from these tests is very small and there's little evidence of long-term adverse consequences.

items.subtopic: ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION
items.statement: False-positive results in screening mammography occur when an abnormality identified is not malignant.
items.explanation: The passage states that a false-positive result occurs when an abnormality is identified that is not malignant.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · ASSESSING THE HARMS OF SCREENING AND PREVENTION

items.statement: The USPSTF recommends screening for breast cancer in women under forty.
items.explanation: Consensus is lower for breast cancer screening among women in their forties.

items.statement: Flexible sigmoidoscopy reduces colon cancer deaths by approximately 40–60%.
items.explanation: The passage states flexible sigmoidoscopy reduces colon cancer deaths by ~40–60%.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · WEIGHING THE BENEFITS AND HARMS

items.statement: The USPSTF recommends a wide range of counseling activities for disease prevention.
items.explanation: The passage notes that the evidence was conclusive to recommend only a relatively small set of counseling activities.

items.subtopic: COUNSELING ON HEALTHY BEHAVIORS | IMPLEMENTING DISEASE PREVENTION AND SCREENING
items.statement: For patients with advanced diseases and limited life expectancy, focusing on screening procedures is beneficial.
items.explanation: The passage suggests shifting the focus from screening to interventions more likely to affect quality and length of life for these patients.

items.assertion: Polygenic risk scores can identify individuals at high risk for coronary heart disease.
items.reason: These scores combine information about variants across hundreds of genes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.assertion: Prophylactic mastectomy greatly reduces breast cancer risk for women with BRCA mutations.
items.reason: This procedure is recommended for all women regardless of genetic status.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as it's specific to those with BRCA mutations.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · Screening and Prevention of Disease

items.subtopic: TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING
items.assertion: Low-dose chest CT scanning has reduced lung cancer mortality.
items.reason: It can detect tumors earlier than traditional methods like chest x-ray.
items.explanation: Early detection through low-dose chest CT allows for intervention before the disease becomes incurable, reducing mortality.
items.reference: Screening and Prevention of Disease · TABLE 6-1 Principles of Screening | BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SCREENING

items.explanation: Economic costs are a critical consideration in screening programs, balancing them with available resources and alternative strategies.

items.explanation: The assertion is false because Pap smears do not benefit the majority who never develop cervical cancer.

items.reason: Long-term follow-up is needed to determine the true incidence of disease over time in an unscreened population.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why assessing overdiagnosis requires long-term follow-up.

items.assertion: Shared decision-making is crucial for preventive services.
items.reason: The benefit-to-harm ratio can be uncertain for specific populations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why shared decision-making is important.

items.assertion: Colonoscopy offers no additional benefit over flexible sigmoidoscopy.
